title: Leukaemia
body_html:
Background
Leukaemias are clonal malignant disorders of the bone marrow characterised by the uncontrolled accumulation of abnormal white blood cells in the marrow and peripheral blood. They are broadly classified into:
  • Acute leukaemias: Rapid onset and aggressive progression, involving immature precursors (blasts).
  • Chronic leukaemias: Slower progression, often involving more mature or partially differentiated cells.

Pathophysiology of acute and chronic leukaemias
Acute leukaemias arise due to malignant transformation of haemopoietic stem or progenitor cells, leading to:
  • Increased proliferation
  • Reduced apoptosis
  • Blocked cellular differentiation

This results in the accumulation of blast cells in the bone marrow. Acute leukaemia is typically defined by >20% blasts in the marrow (WHO classification) or presence of specific genetic abnormalities. Bone marrow failure is the dominant feature, with variable organ infiltration.

Chronic leukaemias involve the clonal proliferation of mature or maturing (differentiated) blood cells, rather than blasts. Unlike acute leukaemias, they are not defined by blast percentage, but typically show:
  • A blast count <10% in both bone marrow and peripheral blood
  • A more indolent clinical course, often with subtle or asymptomatic presentation
  • The presence of mature or maturing cells on blood film and marrow
  • Characteristic cytogenetic or molecular abnormalities, such as the Philadelphia chromosome (BCR-ABL1) in CML, and deletion 13q, trisomy 12, or IGHV mutation status in CLL.

SubtypeDefining featuresBlast countCell maturityKey marker(s)
AMLRapid onset; myeloid blasts>20%Immature (blasts)Auer rods, MPO+, CD13/CD33+
ALLRapid onset; lymphoid blasts>20%Immature (blasts)TdT+, CD10/CD19+ (B-ALL), CD3+ (T-ALL)
CMLIndolent; granulocytic proliferation<10%Mature and maturing myeloid cellsBCR-ABL1 (Philadelphia chromosome)
CLLIndolent; clonal B-cell lymphocytosis<10%Small, mature lymphocytesCD5+, CD19+, CD23+, weak sIg


Clinical features
Acute leukaemia presents with a wide spectrum of clinical features, ranging from subtle abnormalities on routine blood tests to life-threatening emergencies. Most patients present acutely unwell, often to the emergency department, with symptoms or complications arising from marrow failure or leukemic infiltration. In some cases, the diagnosis is made incidentally, following investigation of isolated cytopaenias in otherwise asymptomatic patients.

Symptoms of marrow failure
These symptoms reflect the impaired production of normal blood cells:
  • Anaemia: fatigue, malaise, shortness of breath, dizziness, or syncope
  • Thrombocytopaenia: easy bruising, petechiae, mucosal bleeding (e.g., gums, nosebleeds)
  • Neutropenia / dysfunctional white cells: recurrent infections, fevers, sepsis — many patients report a history of unusually frequent or severe infections prior to diagnosis

Symptoms related to high WCC
Leukostasis is a hyperviscosity syndrome caused by a high burden of circulating blasts. Symptoms include:
  • Headache
  • Visual disturbances
  • Stroke-like symptoms
  • Respiratory distress or failure
  • Tumour lysis syndrome: results from rapid cell breakdown, often triggered by treatment or high disease burden. Features include: renal failure, hyperkalaemia, hyperphosphataemia, hypocalcaemia, and hyperuricaemia (may cause gout).

Symptoms due to leukaemic infiltration
Leukaemic cells can infiltrate non-haematopoietic tissues, leading to diverse and sometimes organ-specific symptoms:
  • Central nervous system (CNS) involvement (more common in ALL): headache, cranial nerve palsies, signs of aseptic meningitis
  • Skin: leukaemia cutis (violaceous or nodular skin lesions)
  • Gingival hypertrophy: particularly associated with monocytic subtypes of AML
  • Bone and joint pain: especially in children; may occur even in the absence of cytopaenias
  • Mediastinal mass: may cause superior vena cava (SVC) obstruction, particularly in T-cell ALL
  • Scrotal involvement: testicular swelling due to infiltration




Acute Myeloid Leukaemia (AML)
Epidemiology:
  • Most common acute leukaemia in adults
  • Median onset ~65 years
  • Rare in children (~10-15%)

Clinical features:
  • Symptoms of bone marrow failure: anaemia, infection, bleeding
  • Organ infiltration (e.g. skin, gums)

Investigations:
  • Normochromic normocytic anaemia, thrombocytopenia
  • Elevated or normal WBCs with circulating blasts
  • Hypercellular marrow with >20% myeloblasts

Management:
  • Supportive: transfusions, infection control, tumour lysis prevention
  • Specific: chemotherapy ± stem cell transplant
  • Remission defined by <5% blasts, recovery of counts

Prognosis:
  • Dependent on age and cytogenetics
  • ~30–40% long-term cure in younger adults; <10% in patients >70 years.


Acute Lymphoblastic Leukaemia (ALL)
Epidemiology:
  • Most common childhood cancer
  • Peak incidence 3–7 years
  • Secondary rise in adults >40 years

Clinical features:
  • Bone marrow failure: pallor, fever, bleeding
  • Organ infiltration: lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, bone pain, CNS involvement (headache, cranial nerve signs), testicular involvement

Investigations:
  • Anaemia, thrombocytopenia, blast cells on film
  • WCC may be high due to blasts, but neutropenia is often present due to marrow suppression
  • > 20% lymphoblasts in marrow
  • CSF analysis with intrathecal chemotherapy
  • Biochemical: ↑LDH, uric acid; imaging for masses

Management:
  • Children: chemotherapy ± radiotherapy tailored to age/gender/WBC count
  • Adults: similar, but with lower cure rates

Prognosis:
  • ~90% cure rate in children; ~5–10% in adults >70 years.


Chronic Myeloid Leukaemia (CML)
Incidence:
  • ~15% of leukaemias
  • Peak age 40–60 years.
  • Associated with the Philadelphia chromosome (BCR-ABL1 fusion gene).

Clinical features:
Often asymptomatic. Symptomatic patients may report:
  • Weight loss, night sweats, fatigue
  • Splenomegaly
  • Gout/uric acid symptoms

Investigations:
  • Marked leucocytosis (neutrophils, myelocytes)
  • Basophilia, anaemia, possible thrombocytosis
  • Ph+ chromosome in ~95% on cytogenetics

Management:
  • Tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs e.g. imatinib)
  • Allogeneic stem cell transplant in select cases

Prognosis:
  • Excellent with TKI therapy—>90% long-term disease control.


Chronic Lymphocytic Leukaemia (CLL)
Epidemiology:
  • Most common leukaemia in the West
  • Peak age 60–80 years.
  • Male predominance (2:1)

Clinical features:
  • Often incidental diagnosis
  • Lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly
  • Anaemia, thrombocytopenia
  • Recurrent infections due to immunosuppression

Investigations:
  • Lymphocytosis with clonal B-cells
  • Normocytic anaemia, thrombocytopenia
  • Marrow replacement by lymphocytes
  • ↓Immunoglobulins, +autoimmune haemolysis in some

Management:
  • Conservative in early stages
  • Chemotherapy and anti-CD20 antibodies if symptomatic or progressive

Prognosis:
Variable course. Many live for years without treatment; disease is typically incurable.


Summary Table
TypeMolecular/Cell LineageKey FeaturesTypical Management
Acute Lymphoblastic Leukaemia (ALL)Lymphoid progenitor (mostly B-cell)Rapid onset cytopenias, lymphadenopathy, CNS/testicular involvementMulti-phase chemotherapy with CNS prophylaxis
Acute Myeloid Leukaemia (AML)Myeloid blastsPancytopenia, APL subtype with DIC riskInduction ± ATRA (APL), consolidation ± transplant
Chronic Lymphocytic Leukaemia (CLL)Mature B lymphocytesOften incidental, indolent courseObservation or targeted agents if symptomatic
Chronic Myeloid Leukaemia (CML)Myeloid stem cell with BCR-ABL1Elevated WBC, splenomegaly, chronic phase often asymptomaticTKI therapy, transplant if resistant

ED considerations
Acute leukaemias may present as life-threatening emergencies in the ED. Key complications include febrile neutropenia, tumour lysis syndrome, leukostasis, and CNS involvement. Prompt supportive care and urgent haematology referral are critical.

Key Points
ALL & AML present quickly and can be rapidly fatal if left untreated
CLL & CML typically there is adequate bone marrow reserve as the neoplastic elements coexist with normal haematopoetic cells.

CLL and CML can transform into higher grade malignancies (ALL and AML respectively) and do so in 1/3 and 2/3 of cases respectively.

ALL: Peak at 2-5 years of age. Most common leukaemia in children. 90% children cured
AML: More common in adults. 5 year survival rates 40%
CLL: Mainly affects adults 5 year survival 60%
CML: Mainly affects adults 5 year survival 90%

Pathology
